content:
# 3 John — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** 3 John (Greek: Iōannou G) | **Chapters:** 1

## At a Glance
- **Author:** "The elder" (ho presbyteros, v. 1) — the same designation as 2 John.
- **Date:** c. 90–110 CE.
- **Audience:** Gaius (v. 1) — a specific individual, otherwise unknown. The name was extremely common in the Roman world.
- **Genre:** Personal letter — the most genuinely personal letter in the NT after Philemon.
- **Timeline:** c. 90–110 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** 3 John is a personal letter praising Gaius for his hospitality to traveling missionaries, condemning Diotrephes for refusing the elder's authority, and commending Demetrius — a window into the politics of early church leadership.

## The World Behind the Text
The letter reveals a power struggle. Diotrephes "loves to be first" (philoprōteuōn, v. 9) and refuses to welcome the elder's representatives. He "spreads malicious nonsense" about the elder and expels from the church those who do welcome the missionaries (v. 10). This is evidence of competing authority structures in early Christianity — the elder's itinerant, trans-local authority versus Diotrephes' local, congregational control.

## Why This Book Was Written
The elder writes to Gaius to secure hospitality for traveling missionaries (vv. 5–8), to complain about Diotrephes (vv. 9–10), and to commend Demetrius (v. 12). The letter provides a rare glimpse of early church conflict that is not about doctrine but about authority and hospitality.

## How to Read This Book
**vv. 1–4:** Greeting and commendation of Gaius. **vv. 5–8:** Hospitality to traveling brothers. **vv. 9–10:** The Diotrephes problem. **vv. 11–12:** Commendation of Demetrius. **vv. 13–15:** Closing — "I have much to write you, but I do not want to do so with pen and ink. I hope to see you soon, and we will talk face to face" (vv. 13–14).

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
3 John, 2 John, and the Didache together illuminate the early Christian practice of itinerant ministry and the tensions it created. The Didache (11–13) provides rules for distinguishing genuine from fraudulent traveling prophets — suggesting the problem was widespread.

The Diotrephes incident may reflect the transition from charismatic/apostolic authority (the elder) to institutional/monarchical authority (a local leader who resists outside control). This transition, visible across the late 1st and early 2nd centuries (the Pastoral Epistles, Ignatius's letters), was a defining development in early Christianity.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **3 John contains no theological content** — no christology, no soteriology, no ethics. It is entirely about interpersonal relationships, hospitality, and authority. Its canonical status was debated precisely because of its seemingly mundane content.
- **"Loves to be first" (philoprōteuōn, v. 9)** is a hapax legomenon in the NT — a unique word that may characterize Diotrephes as power-seeking or may describe his role as a leader who oversteps.
- **"We ought to show hospitality to such people so that we may work together for the truth" (v. 8)** — the Greek synergoi ("fellow workers/co-workers") for those who support itinerant missionaries by offering hospitality.
- **The letter's survival** is remarkable given its brevity and personal nature — it was preserved because of its connection to the Johannine collection.
- **Codex Sinaiticus (4th century)** is the earliest complete witness. No pre-4th century papyrus of 3 John survives.

## Timeline
- **c. 90–110 CE:** Composition
- **c. 100 CE:** Didache — comparable evidence about itinerant ministry
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus — earliest complete manuscript

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
